# Load the dataset
wine_data <- read.csv("C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/winequality-white.csv", sep = ";")

EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS

# View the structure and summary of the dataset
str(wine_data)
## 'data.frame':    4898 obs. of  12 variables:
##  $ fixed.acidity       : num  7 6.3 8.1 7.2 7.2 8.1 6.2 7 6.3 8.1 ...
##  $ volatile.acidity    : num  0.27 0.3 0.28 0.23 0.23 0.28 0.32 0.27 0.3 0.22 ...
##  $ citric.acid         : num  0.36 0.34 0.4 0.32 0.32 0.4 0.16 0.36 0.34 0.43 ...
##  $ residual.sugar      : num  20.7 1.6 6.9 8.5 8.5 6.9 7 20.7 1.6 1.5 ...
##  $ chlorides           : num  0.045 0.049 0.05 0.058 0.058 0.05 0.045 0.045 0.049 0.044 ...
##  $ free.sulfur.dioxide : num  45 14 30 47 47 30 30 45 14 28 ...
##  $ total.sulfur.dioxide: num  170 132 97 186 186 97 136 170 132 129 ...
##  $ density             : num  1.001 0.994 0.995 0.996 0.996 ...
##  $ pH                  : num  3 3.3 3.26 3.19 3.19 3.26 3.18 3 3.3 3.22 ...
##  $ sulphates           : num  0.45 0.49 0.44 0.4 0.4 0.44 0.47 0.45 0.49 0.45 ...
##  $ alcohol             : num  8.8 9.5 10.1 9.9 9.9 10.1 9.6 8.8 9.5 11 ...
##  $ quality             : int  6 6 6 6 6 6 6 6 6 6 ...
summary(wine_data)
##  fixed.acidity    volatile.acidity  citric.acid     residual.sugar  
##  Min.   : 3.800   Min.   :0.0800   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   : 0.600  
##  1st Qu.: 6.300   1st Qu.:0.2100   1st Qu.:0.2700   1st Qu.: 1.700  
##  Median : 6.800   Median :0.2600   Median :0.3200   Median : 5.200  
##  Mean   : 6.855   Mean   :0.2782   Mean   :0.3342   Mean   : 6.391  
##  3rd Qu.: 7.300   3rd Qu.:0.3200   3rd Qu.:0.3900   3rd Qu.: 9.900  
##  Max.   :14.200   Max.   :1.1000   Max.   :1.6600   Max.   :65.800  
##    chlorides       free.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide    density      
##  Min.   :0.00900   Min.   :  2.00      Min.   :  9.0        Min.   :0.9871  
##  1st Qu.:0.03600   1st Qu.: 23.00      1st Qu.:108.0        1st Qu.:0.9917  
##  Median :0.04300   Median : 34.00      Median :134.0        Median :0.9937  
##  Mean   :0.04577   Mean   : 35.31      Mean   :138.4        Mean   :0.9940  
##  3rd Qu.:0.05000   3rd Qu.: 46.00      3rd Qu.:167.0        3rd Qu.:0.9961  
##  Max.   :0.34600   Max.   :289.00      Max.   :440.0        Max.   :1.0390  
##        pH          sulphates         alcohol         quality     
##  Min.   :2.720   Min.   :0.2200   Min.   : 8.00   Min.   :3.000  
##  1st Qu.:3.090   1st Qu.:0.4100   1st Qu.: 9.50   1st Qu.:5.000  
##  Median :3.180   Median :0.4700   Median :10.40   Median :6.000  
##  Mean   :3.188   Mean   :0.4898   Mean   :10.51   Mean   :5.878  
##  3rd Qu.:3.280   3rd Qu.:0.5500   3rd Qu.:11.40   3rd Qu.:6.000  
##  Max.   :3.820   Max.   :1.0800   Max.   :14.20   Max.   :9.000
#Show the first rows of the data
head(wine_data)
##   fixed.acidity volatile.acidity citric.acid residual.sugar chlorides
## 1           7.0             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
## 2           6.3             0.30        0.34            1.6     0.049
## 3           8.1             0.28        0.40            6.9     0.050
## 4           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
## 5           7.2             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
## 6           8.1             0.28        0.40            6.9     0.050
##   free.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide density   pH sulphates alcohol
## 1                  45                  170  1.0010 3.00      0.45     8.8
## 2                  14                  132  0.9940 3.30      0.49     9.5
## 3                  30                   97  0.9951 3.26      0.44    10.1
## 4                  47                  186  0.9956 3.19      0.40     9.9
## 5                  47                  186  0.9956 3.19      0.40     9.9

## 1       6
## 2       6
## 3       6
## 4       6
## 5       6
## 6       6
# Histogram of quality
ggplot(wine_data, aes(x = quality)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = 'blue', color = 'black') +
  labs(title = 'Distribution of Wine Quality', x = 'Quality', y = 'Frequency')

# Correlation plot
corr_matrix <- cor(wine_data[, -ncol(wine_data)])
corrplot::corrplot(corr_matrix, method = "circle", type = "upper",
                   tl.col = "black", tl.cex = 0.8, number.cex = 0.7)

DATA CLEANING AND PREPARATION

# Removing duplicates
wine_data <- unique(wine_data)

# Handling missing values
wine_data <- wine_data %>% na.omit()
#Transforming quality as binary variable
wine_data$quality <- ifelse(wine_data$quality >= 6, 1, 0)

#Save the cleaned dataset
#wine_data_cleaned <- write.csv(wine_data, "C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_data_cleaned.csv")
#wine_data <- read.csv("C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_data_cleaned.csv")

#Splitting the data into Training and Testing sets
set.seed(123)  # For reproducibility
trainIndex <- createDataPartition(wine_data$quality, p = .8,
                                  list = FALSE,
                                  times = 1)
wine_train <- wine_data[trainIndex,]
wine_test <- wine_data[-trainIndex,]

#Saving
#write.csv(wine_train, "C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_train.csv", row.names = FALSE)
#write.csv(wine_test, "C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_test.csv", row.names = FALSE)

LOGISTIC REGRESSION

# Estimate the logistic model
model_glm <- glm(formula = quality ~ .,
                 data = wine_train, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
# View summary statistics
print(summary(model_glm))
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = quality ~ ., family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
##     data = wine_train)
## 
## Coefficients:
##                        Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)           2.066e+02  8.447e+01   2.446   0.0144 *  
## fixed.acidity        -2.586e-02  8.527e-02  -0.303   0.7617    
## volatile.acidity     -6.285e+00  5.193e-01 -12.103  < 2e-16 ***
## citric.acid           4.136e-02  3.843e-01   0.108   0.9143    
## residual.sugar        1.375e-01  3.248e-02   4.233 2.30e-05 ***
## chlorides             5.164e-01  2.222e+00   0.232   0.8162    
## free.sulfur.dioxide   9.346e-03  3.530e-03   2.648   0.0081 ** 
## total.sulfur.dioxide -1.084e-03  1.517e-03  -0.715   0.4749    
## density              -2.198e+02  8.561e+01  -2.567   0.0103 *  
## pH                    1.286e+00  4.431e-01   2.903   0.0037 ** 
## sulphates             2.299e+00  4.535e-01   5.071 3.97e-07 ***
## alcohol               7.861e-01  1.123e-01   7.002 2.53e-12 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 4056.9  on 3168  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 3169.3  on 3157  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 3193.3
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5

The model suggests that volatile acidity, residual sugar, sulphates, alcohol, pH, and density are key factors influencing wine quality, with volatile acidity, sulphates, and alcohol showing particularly strong effects.

The substantial reduction from null deviance (4056.9) to residual deviance (3169.3) upon adding the predictors indicates that the model with predictors provides a significantly better fit.

with(model_glm, pchisq(null.deviance - deviance, df.null - df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE))
## [1] 2.850641e-183

This result suggests that the improvements in model fit (reduction in deviance) achieved by including predictors in the model are highly significant.

TREE MODEL

Base Tree Construction

# Build a decision tree model using rpart
wine_train$quality <- as.factor(wine_train$quality)
model_tree <- rpart(data = wine_train,formula=quality~alcohol+volatile.acidity+free.sulfur.dioxide+chlorides+sulphates+pH, method = "class")
# Plot the decision tree model using prp
prp(model_tree,extra=2, main = "Base tree for model")

Result:

-Alcohol and volatile acidity are the most influential, appearing as primary decision nodes.

-Alcohol differentiates at a very fundamental level, while volatile acidity helps refine classifications particularly at lower alcohol levels.

# Print a  plot data on tree
printcp(model_tree)
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = quality ~ alcohol + volatile.acidity + free.sulfur.dioxide + 
##     chlorides + sulphates + pH, data = wine_train, method = "class")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] alcohol             chlorides           free.sulfur.dioxide
## [4] sulphates           volatile.acidity   
## 
## Root node error: 1073/3169 = 0.33859
## 
## n= 3169 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.121622      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.024828
## 2 0.016309      2   0.75676 0.78006 0.023129
## 3 0.010718      4   0.72414 0.76142 0.022949
## 4 0.010252      6   0.70270 0.76142 0.022949
## 5 0.010000      8   0.68220 0.76421 0.022977
plotcp(model_tree)

Complexity and Pruning

To make the tree less complex we can select a complexity parameter that minimizes the xerror. This will yield a simpler model

# Determine the minimum complexity parameter (cp) for pruning
# cp parameter: captures model complexity

min_cp <- model_tree$cptable[which.min(model_tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
# Prune the decision tree model using the minimum cp
model_tree_2 <- prune(model_tree, cp = min_cp )
# Plot the pruned decision tree model using prp.
prp(model_tree_2, main = "Pruned base tree")

# Print a plot data on tree 2
printcp(model_tree_2)
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = quality ~ alcohol + volatile.acidity + free.sulfur.dioxide + 
##     chlorides + sulphates + pH, data = wine_train, method = "class")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] alcohol             free.sulfur.dioxide volatile.acidity   
## 
## Root node error: 1073/3169 = 0.33859
## 
## n= 3169 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.121622      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.024828
## 2 0.016309      2   0.75676 0.78006 0.023129
## 3 0.010718      4   0.72414 0.76142 0.022949
plotcp(model_tree_2)

### Accuracy Evaluation
# Calculate the mean accuracy of the 2 model predictions

mean(predict(model_tree, wine_train, type = "class")==wine_train$quality)
## [1] 0.7690123
mean(predict(model_tree_2, wine_train, type = "class")==wine_train$quality)
## [1] 0.7548122
# Result is that pruned tree makes somewhat less accurate predictions.Therefore we will choose as representative not-pruned model :

model_trees<-model_tree

Exploration of Variable Combinations

# Build a decision tree model using rpart
model_tree_3 <- rpart(data = wine_train, formula = quality ~ alcohol+ pH, method = "class")
# Plot the decision tree model using prp
prp(model_tree_3, extra= 2 , main = "Tree for Alcohol and pH")

# Determine the minimum complexity parameter (cp) for pruning
min_cp <- model_tree_3$cptable[which.min(model_tree_3$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
# Prune the decision tree model using the minimum cp
model_tree_4 <- prune(model_tree_3, cp = min_cp)
#Show the mean of the pruned model in order to compare it later
mean(predict(model_tree_4, wine_train, type = "class") == wine_train$quality)
## [1] 0.7194699
# Create a scatter plot with decision tree boundaries for Alcohol and pH variables
ggplot(data = wine_train, aes(x = alcohol, y = pH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = quality)) +
  geom_parttree(data = model_tree_4, aes(fill = quality), alpha = 0.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black", "deepskyblue2")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("black", "deepskyblue2")) +
  guides(color = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "Alcohol",
    y = "pH",
    color = "quality",
    title = "Scatter plot of alcohol and pH showing quality and tree estimation of quality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_line(color = "#000000"))

Result : There is a noticeable cluster of lower quality wines at lower pH and lower alcohol levels.

However pH impacts wine quality classification primarily at lower levels of alcohol.

As alcohol content increases, the relative impact of pH diminishes, suggesting that alcohol content is a stronger predictor of quality in this model.

# Clean up
rm(list = c("min_cp", "model_tree", "model_tree_2", "model_tree_3", "model_tree_4"))

Investigating hyperparameters for the tree model

# Build a decision tree model with the specified parameters
rpart(data=wine_train,
      formula = quality ~ alcohol+pH,
      method = "class",
      cp = 0.01,              # complexity parameter
      maxdepth = 10,          # maximum tree depth
      minbucket = 5)          # min number of observations in leaves
## n= 3169 
## 
## node), split, n, loss, yval, (yprob)
##       * denotes terminal node
## 
##  1) root 3169 1073 1 (0.3385926 0.6614074)  
##    2) alcohol< 10.01667 1249  560 0 (0.5516413 0.4483587)  
##      4) alcohol< 9.45 684  270 0 (0.6052632 0.3947368) *
##      5) alcohol>=9.45 565  275 1 (0.4867257 0.5132743)  
##       10) pH< 3.245 387  179 0 (0.5374677 0.4625323)  
##         20) alcohol< 9.85 262  111 0 (0.5763359 0.4236641) *
##         21) alcohol>=9.85 125   57 1 (0.4560000 0.5440000) *
##       11) pH>=3.245 178   67 1 (0.3764045 0.6235955) *
##    3) alcohol>=10.01667 1920  384 1 (0.2000000 0.8000000) *
# Define the values for minimum observations, maximum depth, and complexity parameter (cp)
min_obs <- c(5,10,15,20)
max_depth <- c(1,5,10,15)
cp_val <- c(0.001,0.01,0.05,0.1)

# Create an empty data frame to store the results
results <- data.frame()
# Iterate over the combinations of min_obs, max_depth, and cp_val
for (i in 1:length(min_obs)){
  for (j in 1:length(max_depth)){
    for (k in 1:length(cp_val)){

      #  Build a decision tree model with the specified parameters
      model <- rpart(data=wine_train, formula = quality ~ alcohol + pH, method = "class",
                     cp = cp_val[k],
                     maxdepth = max_depth[j],
                     minbucket = min_obs[i])
      # Calculate the accuracy of the model predictions
      acc <- mean(predict(model, wine_train, type = "class")==wine_train$quality)
      # Append the accuracy and parameter values to the results data frame
      results <- rbind(results, c(acc,min_obs[i],max_depth[j],cp_val[k]))
    }
  }
}
# Set column names for the results data frame
colnames(results) <- c("accuracy","min_obs","max_depth","cp")

# Create a 3D scatter plot using scatter3D
scatter3D(x=results$min_obs, y=results$max_depth, z=results$cp,
          theta=30 ,phi=10,
          xlab="Min Obs", ylab="Max Depth", zlab="CP",
          ticktype="detailed",
          pch = 19,
          cex.axis=0.75, cex.lab=0.75, expand=0.75,
          colvar = results$accuracy, col = viridis(100))

# This plot serves as a tool for selecting the most effective model parameters by identifying the combinations that yield the highest predictive accuracy.
# Select the optimal parameter combination with the highest accuracy
opt_params <- results[which.max(results$accuracy),]
opt_params
##     accuracy min_obs max_depth    cp
## 13 0.7541811       5        15 0.001
# Build a decision tree model using the optimal parameters
model_tree_5 <- rpart(data=wine_train, formula = quality~alcohol+pH,
                      method = "class",
                      cp = opt_params$cp,
                      maxdepth = opt_params$max_depth,
                      minbucket = opt_params$min_obs)
# Calculate the mean accuracy of the model predictions
mean(predict(model_tree_5,wine_train, type = "class") == wine_train$quality)
## [1] 0.7541811
## Result : Comparing model 4  to the tree with optimal parameter settings from our grid search ( model 5),
# the last one has slighlty better performance : 4% difference.
# Create a scatter plot with decision tree boundaries for Alcohol and pH variables
ggplot(data = wine_train, aes(x = alcohol, y = pH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = quality)) +
  geom_parttree(data = model_tree_5, aes(fill = quality), alpha = 0.1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("black", "deepskyblue2")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("black", "deepskyblue2")) +
  guides(color = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "Alcohol",
    y = "pH",
    color = "quality",
    title = "Scatter plot of alcohol and pH showing quality and tree estimation of quality"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_line(color = "#000000"))

Result : When we rerun the grid search with optimum parameters, we find essentially perfect fits and decision boundaries that carve out almost every data point.

# Clean up
rm(list = c("acc", "cp_val", "i", "j", "k", "max_depth", "min_obs", "results", "opt_params", "model"))

Neural Network

set.seed(12345)

#Setting up training data
X <- as.matrix(wine_train[,c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar", "chlorides", "free.sulfur.dioxide", "total.sulfur.dioxide", "density", "pH", "sulphates", "alcohol")])
y <- as.matrix(as.numeric(as.character(wine_train$quality)))

# Creating and adapting the normalizing layer
normalize <- layer_normalization()
normalize %>% adapt(X) #To store feature mean and s.d. with the layer

print(normalize$mean)
## tf.Tensor(
## [[6.8533769e+00 2.8006479e-01 3.3451885e-01 5.9170732e+00 4.5296934e-02
##   3.4883251e+01 1.3731557e+02 9.9381107e-01 3.1957428e+00 4.9297884e-01
##   1.0589120e+01]], shape=(1, 11), dtype=float32)
model_neural <- keras_model_sequential() #Creating a sequential model

model_neural %>%
  normalize() %>%   #Apply normalization to Layer #1

  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_1", units = 32, activation = 'relu') %>%   
  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_2", units = 16, activation = 'relu') %>%  

   layer_dense(name = "OutputLayer", units = 1)   

summary(model_neural)
## Model: "sequential"
## ________________________________________________________________________________
##  Layer (type)                  Output Shape               Param #    Trainable  
## ================================================================================
##  normalization (Normalization  (None, 11)                 23         Y          
##  )                                                                              
##  HiddenLayer_1 (Dense)         (None, 32)                 384        Y          
##  HiddenLayer_2 (Dense)         (None, 16)                 528        Y          
##  OutputLayer (Dense)           (None, 1)                  17         Y          
## ================================================================================
## Total params: 952 (3.72 KB)
## Trainable params: 929 (3.63 KB)
## Non-trainable params: 23 (96.00 Byte)
## ________________________________________________________________________________
# Compiling the model
model_neural %>% compile(
  loss = "MSE",
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  metrics = 'accuracy'
)

#Training the model
model_neural %>% fit(
  x = X, 
  y = y, 
  epochs = 100,
  verbose = 0
)

model_neural %>% evaluate(X, y) #Evaluate the model on the data
## 100/100 - 0s - loss: 0.1192 - accuracy: 0.8331 - 359ms/epoch - 4ms/step
##      loss  accuracy 
## 0.1192143 0.8330704
rm(list = c("X", "y"))

Investigating Hyperparameters: Neural Networks

# values defined
learning_rate <- c(0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1)
width <- c(8, 16, 24)
length <- c(1, 3, 5)

# Creating a dataframe for results
results <- data.frame()

#setting up Data
X <- as.matrix(wine_train[,c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar", "chlorides", "free.sulfur.dioxide", "total.sulfur.dioxide", "density", "pH", "sulphates", "alcohol")])
y <- as.matrix(as.numeric(as.character(wine_train$quality)))

#iterating over all combinations
for (i in 1:length(learning_rate)) {
  for (j in 1:length(width)) {
    for (k in 1:length(length)) {
      
      if (length[k] == 1) {
        normalize_i <- layer_normalization()
        normalize_i %>% adapt(X)
        
        model_neural_i <- keras_model_sequential()
        
        model_neural_i %>%
          normalize_i %>%
          layer_dense(name = "hidden_1", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
            layer_dense(name = "Output_layer", units = 1)
        model_neural_i %>% compile(loss = "MSE", optimizer_adam(learning_rate = learning_rate[i]), metrics = "accuracy")
        model_neural_i %>% fit(x=X, y=y, epochs = 100, verbose = 0)
      } else if (length[k] == 3) {
        normalize_i <- layer_normalization()
        normalize_i %>% adapt(X)
        
        model_neural_i <- keras_model_sequential()
        
        model_neural_i %>%
          normalize_i() %>%
          
          layer_dense(name = "Hidden_1", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "Hidden_2", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "Hidden_3", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          
            layer_dense(name = "Output_layer", units = 1)
        
        model_neural_i %>% compile(loss="MSE", optimizer=optimizer_adam(learning_rate = learning_rate[i]), metrics="accuracy")
        
        model_neural_i %>% fit(x = X, y = y, epochs = 100, verbose = 0)
      } else if (length[k] == 5) {
        
        normalize_i <- layer_normalization()
        normalize_i %>% adapt(X)
        
        model_neural_i <- keras_model_sequential()
        
        model_neural_i %>%
          normalize_i() %>%
          
          layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_1", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_2", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_3", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_4", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_5", units = width[j], activation = 'relu') %>%
          
            layer_dense(name = "OutputLayer", units = 1)
        
        model_neural_i %>% compile(loss="MSE", optimizer=optimizer_adam(learning_rate = learning_rate[i]), metrics="Accuracy")
        model_neural_i %>% fit(x = X, y = y, epochs = 100, verbose = 0)
      }
      
      #Evaluating accuracy of the models
      eval <- model_neural_i %>% evaluate(X, y, verbose = 0)
      acc <- eval[2]
      
      #collecting data into results dataframe
      results <- rbind(results, c(acc, learning_rate[i], width[j], length[k]))
    }
  }
}

#Setting column names
colnames(results) <- c("accuracy", "learning_rate", "width", "length")

#creating 3D scatter plot
plot3D::scatter3D(x = results$learning_rate, y = results$width, z = results$length,
                  theta = 30, phi = 10,
                  xlab = "Learning Rate", ylab = "Width", zlab = "Length",
                  ticktype = "detailed",
                  pch = 19,
                  cex.axis = 0.75, cex.lab = 0.75, expand = 0.75,
                  colvar = results$accuracy, col = viridis(100))

# Choosing the optimal parameters
opt_params <- results[which.max(results$accuracy),]
opt_params
##     accuracy learning_rate width length
## 18 0.9135374         0.001    24      5

The optimal parameters are likely due to over-fitting, as it’s evident from the highest value of width and length chosen. So, we’ll continue with model_neural as the neural network in order to save computational memory.

# Clean up
rm(list = c("X", "y", "i", "j", "k", "width", "length", "learning_rate", "results", "opt_params", "normalize_i", "model_neural_i", "eval"))

ENSEMBLE METHODS

Bagging

-Building a random forest model using the randomForest function.

ntree parameter is set to 500, specifying the number of trees in the random forest.
mtry, which sets the number of variables to be considered at every split is set to the number of columns of the training data minus 1.
randomForest(quality ~ ., data=wine_train,
             mtry=(ncol(wine_train)-1), ntree=500)
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = quality ~ ., data = wine_train, mtry = (ncol(wine_train) -      1), ntree = 500) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 500
## No. of variables tried at each split: 11
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 23.45%
## Confusion matrix:
##     0    1 class.error
## 0 654  419   0.3904939
## 1 324 1772   0.1545802
model_bag <- randomForest(quality ~ ., data=wine_train,
                          mtry=(ncol(wine_train)-1), ntree=500)

Result

The output of the function gives us basic information about the number of trees and the number of variables considered as well as some measure of fit.

-An OOB error of 23.13% suggests that the model misclassifies about 23.13% of the training cases when predicting unseen data, based on OOB samples.

-For class 0, the error rate is about 38.86%, indicating that about 38.86% of the ‘0’ class instances were incorrectly classified. For class 1, the error rate is about 15.08%, meaning about 15.08% of the ‘1’ class instances were incorrectly classified.

Variable Importance for Bagging

# Variable Importance for Bagging
model_rf <- randomForest(quality ~ ., data=wine_train, ntree=500, importance=TRUE)
varImpPlot(model_rf, main="Variable Importance for Bagging ")

Mean Decrease Accuracy Plot shows that alcohol is the most significant variable affecting the model’s accuracy,followed by volatile.acidity and density .

It tends to spread variable importance over more inputs than the right plot .

Mean Decrease Gini Plot also highlights that alcohol is crucial in improving the purity of the model’s decision nodes.

Boosting

model_gbm <- gbm(quality ~ .,
                 data=wine_train,
                 distribution="gaussian", # Distribution of the response variable , sutiable for regression
                 interaction.depth=1, # maximum depth of variable interactions in any tree set to 1
                 n.trees=500, # the total number of trees to fit
                 shrinkage=0.1, # The learning rate , smaller values generally lead to better performance
                 cv.folds = 5 # 5-fold cross-validation should be used.
)
gbm.perf(model_gbm, method = "cv")

## [1] 197

Result

The plot depicts the performance of a gradient boosting model as the number of iterations increases.

The error decreases sharply at first and then stabilizes around 160 iterations.

Variable Importance for Boosting

-For boosted models, we can retrieve the variable importance measures via summary() and the default approach is based on node impurity.

-summary() produces both the relative importance values and a bar plot.

summary(model_gbm, plotit = FALSE)
##                                       var   rel.inf
## alcohol                           alcohol 33.908448
## volatile.acidity         volatile.acidity 13.402972
## residual.sugar             residual.sugar  9.510699
## free.sulfur.dioxide   free.sulfur.dioxide  8.676442
## total.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide  7.178517
## citric.acid                   citric.acid  6.949778
## pH                                     pH  5.610225
## fixed.acidity               fixed.acidity  4.541659
## chlorides                       chlorides  3.956260
## density                           density  3.541027
## sulphates                       sulphates  2.723973

Variables with higher relative importance scores are more crucial for the model to make accurate predictions.

### Organizing  both bagging and boosting output and display them together on one scale.
# Organizing outputs for plotting
dt_varimp <- merge(data.table(importance(model_rf, type = 2), keep.rownames = TRUE),
                   data.table(summary(model_gbm, plotit = FALSE)),
                   by.x = "rn", by.y = "var")
dt_varimp <- setNames(dt_varimp, c("Variable", "Bagging", "Boosting"))
dt_varimp[, Bagging := 100*Bagging/sum(Bagging)]
dt_varimp <- melt(dt_varimp, id.vars = "Variable",
                  measure.vars = c("Bagging","Boosting"),
                  variable.name = "y",
                  value.name = "x")
dt_varimp <- dt_varimp[order(y,-x)]
# Plotting
ggplot(data = dt_varimp) +
  geom_col(aes(y=Variable, x=x, fill=y)) +
  labs(
    x = "Relative Importance",
    y = "Variables") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#f1b147","#4CA7DE")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_line(color = "#000000"),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~y)

Result : The graphs support the above for the most important variables affecting the quality of wine.

rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf"))
## Warning in rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf")): object 'gbm.perf' not found

HYPERPARAMETER SEARCH FOR BOOSTING

#Finding the optimal settings for the hyperparameters:
#Number of trees: n.trees
#Number of splits per tree: interaction.depth
#Minimum number of observations per leaf: n.minobsinnode
#Learning rate: shrinkage

# Define Training Control and Grid

train_settings <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5) # specifying cross-validation and the number of folds.

gbm_grid =  expand.grid(interaction.depth = 1:5,
                        n.trees = c(500,1000,2000,5000),
                        shrinkage = c(0.1, 0.01, 0.001),
                        n.minobsinnode = c(10,20))
# Training the model over the grid .
# This  will iterate over all combinations in gbm_grid and perform cross-validation for each combination

caret_gbm <- train(quality ~ ., data = wine_train,
                   method = "gbm",
                   trControl = train_settings,
                   tuneGrid = gbm_grid,
                   verbose = FALSE)

head(caret_gbm$results)
##    shrinkage interaction.depth n.minobsinnode n.trees  Accuracy     Kappa
## 1      0.001                 1             10     500 0.6614075 0.0000000
## 5      0.001                 1             20     500 0.6614075 0.0000000
## 41     0.010                 1             10     500 0.7529176 0.3970214
## 45     0.010                 1             20     500 0.7500785 0.3890557
## 81     0.100                 1             10     500 0.7557557 0.4371026
## 85     0.100                 1             20     500 0.7592268 0.4423025
##      AccuracySD    KappaSD
## 1  0.0007422251 0.00000000
## 5  0.0007422251 0.00000000
## 41 0.0068050461 0.02172619
## 45 0.0055515070 0.01864116
## 81 0.0074819632 0.02786619
## 85 0.0079830689 0.02480714
plot(caret_gbm)

Result : Interpretation of Key Trends

a.Tree Depth: As the tree depth increases, accuracy initially improves after a certain depth, the increase in accuracy declines slightly in some configurations, suggesting overfitting.

b.Shrinkage : Lower values (like 0.001) show more significant jumps in accuracy , possibly because the slower learning rate combined with more complex models allows better learning without immediately overfitting.

c.N.minobsinnode: Models with n.minobsinnode = 10 consistently perform slightly better. This could be because allowing fewer samples at terminal nodes provides a finer granularity at splits, enhancing the model’s ability to fit complex patterns effectively.

d. n.trees: Increasing the number of trees generally leads to higher accuracies, especially noticeable in settings with lower shrinkage and deeper trees.

PCA

# We standardize the variables first to put them on equal footing.
dt_pca <- data.table(scale(wine_data[, c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar","chlorides","free.sulfur.dioxide","total.sulfur.dioxide","density", "pH","sulphates","alcohol")]))
# To conduct the PCA  we will  use  library "prcomp" which decomposes the covariance matrix for us and supplies other fundamental outputs.
model_pca <- prcomp(dt_pca)
# The summary command the model enables us to dive into our 11 principle componets. We find the share that each PC contributes to explaining the variance of our data in descending order of the eigenvalues.
summary(model_pca)
## Importance of components:
##                           PC1    PC2    PC3     PC4     PC5     PC6     PC7
## Standard deviation     1.7842 1.2635 1.1014 1.01934 0.99052 0.96400 0.84973
## Proportion of Variance 0.2894 0.1451 0.1103 0.09446 0.08919 0.08448 0.06564
## Cumulative Proportion  0.2894 0.4345 0.5448 0.63928 0.72848 0.81296 0.87860
##                            PC8     PC9    PC10    PC11
## Standard deviation     0.77591 0.65485 0.53001 0.15377
## Proportion of Variance 0.05473 0.03898 0.02554 0.00215
## Cumulative Proportion  0.93333 0.97231 0.99785 1.00000
# We  use the fviz functionality of factoextra to produce a scree plot of the above information:
fviz_eig(model_pca, addlabels = TRUE) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

Result : From the plot we see that we explain ~90% of the variance with 7-8 PCs.

# The same above result we can see by implementing  ggplot to generate a plot of the cumulative variance explained as we add more PCs.

df_cumsum <- data.table(Var = model_pca$sdev^2 / sum(model_pca$sdev^2), PC = 1:ncol(dt_pca))

ggplot(data=df_cumsum, aes(y=cumsum(Var), x=PC)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Cumulative Variance Explained",
    x = "Principal Component",
    y = "% Variance Explained") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks=c(1:nrow(df_cumsum))) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

PC Contributions -To investigate how our original variables contribute to the PCs and to possibly interpret these contributions, we can look at the eigenvectors v.

-We find the eigenvectors at model_pca$rotation.

-Matrix of (scaled) eigenvectors: V

-The matrix lists the scaled eigenvectors for each principal component.

-The values in the matrix tell us how much each variable contributes to each principal component.

round(model_pca$rotation,2)
##                        PC1   PC2   PC3   PC4   PC5   PC6   PC7   PC8   PC9
## fixed.acidity        -0.15  0.60 -0.06  0.00  0.26  0.00 -0.21 -0.56 -0.37
## volatile.acidity     -0.03 -0.06  0.67 -0.17  0.38 -0.43  0.29 -0.11  0.18
## citric.acid          -0.15  0.38 -0.46 -0.14  0.00 -0.12  0.71  0.07  0.26
## residual.sugar       -0.42 -0.02  0.20  0.28  0.16  0.26  0.24  0.42 -0.36
## chlorides            -0.21  0.03  0.11 -0.67 -0.49 -0.24 -0.02  0.14 -0.42
## free.sulfur.dioxide  -0.31 -0.26 -0.31  0.34 -0.10 -0.50 -0.14  0.06 -0.13
## total.sulfur.dioxide -0.42 -0.23 -0.12  0.09  0.11 -0.38 -0.06 -0.27  0.11
## density              -0.51 -0.01  0.13 -0.01  0.09  0.35  0.08 -0.05 -0.08
## pH                    0.11 -0.58 -0.15 -0.13  0.00  0.26  0.40 -0.53 -0.27
## sulphates            -0.05 -0.19 -0.35 -0.50  0.66  0.06 -0.26  0.27  0.01
## alcohol               0.43  0.05 -0.08  0.19  0.24 -0.30  0.22  0.19 -0.59
##                       PC10  PC11
## fixed.acidity        -0.14 -0.18
## volatile.acidity     -0.23 -0.02
## citric.acid          -0.05 -0.01
## residual.sugar        0.07 -0.49
## chlorides             0.05 -0.03
## free.sulfur.dioxide  -0.58  0.03
## total.sulfur.dioxide  0.71 -0.03
## density              -0.07  0.76
## pH                   -0.12 -0.15
## sulphates            -0.07 -0.04
## alcohol               0.23  0.37

For example, the entry for “fixed.acidity” in PC1 is -0.15, which means there’s a moderate negative contribution of the variable to the first principal component.

The above eigenvectors take each data point (wine) to the PC coordinate system by multiplying vector x i.e. the variables of one wine, by the matrix of eigenvectors V: xV.

So to look at it we will l take the first row from dt_pca, i.e. the first wine , and multiply it out by the matrix of eigenvectors.

as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*% as.matrix(model_pca$rotation) # It computes the coordinates of the first wine in the new PCA coordinate system using matrix multiplication.
##            PC1       PC2       PC3      PC4      PC5      PC6       PC7
## [1,] -3.862214 0.5110736 0.9060899 1.189798 0.160967 1.203353 0.1347961
##           PC8         PC9       PC10       PC11
## [1,] 1.207803 -0.07746574 0.06864338 0.02154921

The result displays the PCA coordinates for the first wine across eleven principal components (PC1 to PC11).

# In order to not calculate it by hand we use this :
model_pca$x[1,]
##         PC1         PC2         PC3         PC4         PC5         PC6 
## -3.86221405  0.51107362  0.90608987  1.18979761  0.16096702  1.20335327 
##         PC7         PC8         PC9        PC10        PC11 
##  0.13479607  1.20780308 -0.07746574  0.06864338  0.02154921

We find in V above that all variables have a negative contribution to PC1 except alcohol and pH , so our first PC appears to capture this specific trade-off.

PC2 has more mixed performance .

#Score plot that plots wines in only the first two dimensions (PC1 and PC2) , which captures only 43% of the variance.
fviz_pca_ind(model_pca,
             geom.ind = "point",
             col.ind = as.factor(wine_data$quality),
             palette = c("#f1b147", "#4CA7DE")) +
  ggtitle("Score Plot") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))

# To investigate how all of our variables load onto the principle components we can use  a loadings plot.

fviz_pca_var(model_pca,
             col.var="contrib",
             repel = TRUE) +
  theme_minimal()

From this plot we can see that the direction of an arrow indicates how the variable correlates with the principal components.

The length of each arrow indicates the strength of the variable’s contribution to the principal components.

Example : ‘fixed.acidity’ and ‘citric.acid’ seem to indicate strongly in one direction, the same happens with other variables ,except from alcohol and pH which have completely different directions .

“alcohol” have a significant positive contribution along PC1, meanwhile “pH” has a notable negative influence on PC2

Reconstructing Variables

# Insert PCA into our data for continued use
pca <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(dt_pca) %*%
                       as.matrix(model_pca$rotation))

data <- cbind(wine_data, pca)
# Define a function to perform PCA transformations on the test data
apply_pca <- function(data, model) {
  dt_pca <- data.table(scale(data[, c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar","chlorides","free.sulfur.dioxide","total.sulfur.dioxide","density", "pH","sulphates","alcohol")]))

  pca <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(dt_pca) %*%
                         as.matrix(model$rotation))

  return(cbind(data, pca))
}
#Wine 1 Original variables

#x

round(as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,],2)
##        fixed.acidity     volatile.acidity          citric.acid 
##                 0.19                -0.10                 0.21 
##       residual.sugar            chlorides  free.sulfur.dioxide 
##                 3.04                -0.04                 0.59 
## total.sulfur.dioxide              density                   pH 
##                 0.76                 2.48                -1.29 
##            sulphates              alcohol 
##                -0.36                -1.47
#Our matrix of (scaled) eigenvectors V above transformed our original 11 coordinates (variables) into 11 PC coordinates.
# Wine 1   PC coordinates
# x * V
round(as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*%
        as.matrix(model_pca$rotation),2)
##        PC1  PC2  PC3  PC4  PC5 PC6  PC7  PC8   PC9 PC10 PC11
## [1,] -3.86 0.51 0.91 1.19 0.16 1.2 0.13 1.21 -0.08 0.07 0.02
# To reconstruct the original variables we can multiply the PC coordinates by V(transposed):

  # Wine 1  Reconstructed original variables
  # x * V * V(transposed)
  round(as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*%
          as.matrix(model_pca$rotation) %*%
          t(as.matrix(model_pca$rotation)), 2)
##      fixed.acidity volatile.acidity citric.acid residual.sugar chlorides
## [1,]          0.19             -0.1        0.21           3.04     -0.04
##      free.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide density    pH sulphates alcohol
## [1,]                0.59                 0.76    2.48 -1.29     -0.36   -1.47

In order to gain in terms of dimensionality reduction we dont need to keep 11 PC coordinates instead of 11 variables .

We have seen at the very beginning that the first 7 PCs capture nearly 90% of the variance of our data, so we will reduce them to 7.

We will call our eigenvector matrix V with only the first 7 columns Vreduced.

# x * V_reduced
as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*%
  as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:7])
##            PC1       PC2       PC3      PC4      PC5      PC6       PC7
## [1,] -3.862214 0.5110736 0.9060899 1.189798 0.160967 1.203353 0.1347961
# We only need the PC coordinates xVreduced and we can reconstruct the original data.
# This reconstruction is approximate and depends on the number of PCs we use.
# x * V_reduced * V_reducedT

as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*%
  as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:7]) %*%
  t(as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:7]))
##      fixed.acidity volatile.acidity citric.acid residual.sugar  chlorides
## [1,]     0.8509567        0.0590288   0.1463632       2.509052 -0.2400666
##      free.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide  density         pH  sulphates
## [1,]           0.5464081             1.052136 2.525874 -0.6632698 -0.6799189
##        alcohol
## [1,] -1.765457

Comparing the original variable values above with the reconstruction in this way :

We can investigate the total reconstruction error for our one wine depending on the number of eigenvectors we retain in Vreduced

We loop through the reconstruction of wine 1 for 1 to 11 PCs:

results <- list()

for (pcs in 1:11){
  wine_reconstructed <- as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,] %*%
    as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:pcs]) %*%
    t(as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:pcs]))

  results[[pcs]] <- data.table("PCs used" = pcs,
                               "MAE"= sum(abs(as.matrix(dt_pca)[1,]-wine_reconstructed)),
                               wine_reconstructed)
}

dt_reconstruction <- rbindlist(results)
round(dt_reconstruction,2)
##     PCs used   MAE fixed.acidity volatile.acidity citric.acid residual.sugar
##        <num> <num>         <num>            <num>       <num>          <num>
##  1:        1  6.79          0.59             0.10        0.59           1.63
##  2:        2  6.62          0.89             0.07        0.78           1.62
##  3:        3  5.79          0.84             0.68        0.36           1.80
##  4:        4  4.80          0.84             0.48        0.20           2.13
##  5:        5  4.71          0.88             0.54        0.20           2.16
##  6:        6  3.28          0.88             0.02        0.05           2.48
##  7:        7  3.25          0.85             0.06        0.15           2.51
##  8:        8  0.30          0.17            -0.07        0.23           3.02
##  9:        9  0.18          0.20            -0.09        0.21           3.05
## 10:       10  0.05          0.19            -0.10        0.21           3.05
## 11:       11  0.00          0.19            -0.10        0.21           3.04
##     chlorides free.sulfur.dioxide total.sulfur.dioxide density    pH sulphates
##         <num>               <num>                <num>   <num> <num>     <num>
##  1:      0.82                1.19                 1.61    1.98 -0.44      0.18
##  2:      0.83                1.06                 1.49    1.97 -0.74      0.09
##  3:      0.93                0.78                 1.38    2.09 -0.87     -0.23
##  4:      0.14                1.18                 1.49    2.08 -1.03     -0.83
##  5:      0.06                1.16                 1.51    2.09 -1.03     -0.72
##  6:     -0.24                0.57                 1.06    2.52 -0.72     -0.64
##  7:     -0.24                0.55                 1.05    2.53 -0.66     -0.68
##  8:     -0.07                0.62                 0.72    2.47 -1.30     -0.35
##  9:     -0.04                0.63                 0.71    2.47 -1.28     -0.35
## 10:     -0.04                0.59                 0.76    2.47 -1.29     -0.35
## 11:     -0.04                0.59                 0.76    2.48 -1.29     -0.36
##     alcohol
##       <num>
##  1:   -1.66
##  2:   -1.63
##  3:   -1.70
##  4:   -1.48
##  5:   -1.44
##  6:   -1.80
##  7:   -1.77
##  8:   -1.54
##  9:   -1.49
## 10:   -1.48
## 11:   -1.47
# Some of the reconstructed values may differ  from the original values until we use all 11 PCs but this is only one wine.
# The MAE across all data points is not 0 and depends on the number of PCs used.

results <- list()

for (pcs in 1:11){
  wine_reconstructed <- as.matrix(dt_pca) %*%
    as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:pcs]) %*%
    t(as.matrix(model_pca$rotation[,1:pcs]))

  results[[pcs]] <- data.table("PCs used" = pcs,
                               "MAE"= mean(colMeans(abs(as.matrix(dt_pca)-wine_reconstructed))))
}

dt_reconstruction <- rbindlist(results)
round(dt_reconstruction,2)
##     PCs used   MAE
##        <num> <num>
##  1:        1  0.60
##  2:        2  0.54
##  3:        3  0.49
##  4:        4  0.45
##  5:        5  0.39
##  6:        6  0.31
##  7:        7  0.25
##  8:        8  0.18
##  9:        9  0.09
## 10:       10  0.02
## 11:       11  0.00
# Clean up
rm(list = c("dt_pca", "dt_reconstruction", "pca", "wine_reconstructed", "pcs", "results"))

Models with PCA

PCA: Logistic Regression

# Define the logistic regression using PCAs
model_pca_glm <- glm(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7,
                     data = data, family = binomial(link = "logit"))
# Print summary statistic
print(summary(model_pca_glm))
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7, 
##     family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  0.77410    0.03822  20.252  < 2e-16 ***
## PC1          0.40814    0.02232  18.282  < 2e-16 ***
## PC2         -0.10045    0.02953  -3.401 0.000671 ***
## PC3         -0.59008    0.03701 -15.945  < 2e-16 ***
## PC4          0.43397    0.04251  10.209  < 2e-16 ***
## PC5          0.16635    0.04042   4.116 3.86e-05 ***
## PC6         -0.03437    0.04078  -0.843 0.399358    
## PC7          0.17168    0.04540   3.781 0.000156 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 5080.0  on 3960  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 4317.4  on 3953  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 4333.4
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4
# Fit of the model
with(model_pca_glm, pchisq(null.deviance - deviance, df.null - df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE))
## [1] 2.206382e-160
#Result : # The p-value below 0.001 indicates that the model performs better than an empty model.

PCA: Tree

### Base tree
# Build a decision tree model using rpart
data$quality <- as.factor(data$quality)
model_tree<- rpart(data = data, formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7, method = "class")
# Plot the decision tree model using prp, with extra details (extra = 2)
prp(model_tree, extra = 2, main = "Base tree for model")

# Print an plot data on tree
printcp(model_tree)
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + 
##     PC7, data = data, method = "class")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] PC1 PC2 PC3 PC4
## 
## Root node error: 1348/3961 = 0.34032
## 
## n= 3961 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.045005      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.022122
## 2 0.021513      3   0.86499 0.88724 0.021435
## 3 0.012982      4   0.84347 0.86128 0.021252
## 4 0.010000      6   0.81751 0.84125 0.021105
plotcp(model_tree)

# Determine the minimum complexity parameter (cp) for pruning
#cp parameter: captures model complexity
min_cp <- model_tree$cptable[which.min(model_tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
# Prune the decision tree model using the minimum cp
model_tree_2 <- prune(model_tree, cp = min_cp)
# Plot the pruned decision tree model using prp.
prp(model_tree_2, main = "Pruned base tree with less complexity")

# Calculate the mean accuracy of the model predictions
mean(predict(model_tree, data, type = "class")==data$quality)
## [1] 0.7217874
mean(predict(model_tree_2, data, type = "class")==data$quality)
## [1] 0.7217874
model_pca_trees <- model_tree # We will use the not pruned tree since there is no difference for our validation later.
rm(list = c("model_tree", "model_tree_2"))

PCA: Neural Network

set.seed(12345)

#Setting up Data
X <- as.matrix(data[,c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3", "PC4","PC5", "PC6", "PC7")])
y <- as.matrix(as.numeric(as.character(data$quality)))

normalize_pca <- layer_normalization()
normalize_pca %>% adapt(X)

print(normalize_pca$mean)
## tf.Tensor(
## [[-5.5879354e-08  3.3993274e-08 -1.5948899e-08  1.2514647e-08
##   -1.7607817e-09 -1.2747478e-08 -2.3283064e-09]], shape=(1, 7), dtype=float32)
model_pca_neural <- keras_model_sequential()

model_pca_neural %>% 
  normalize_pca() %>%
  
  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_1", units = 32, activation = 'relu') %>%   
  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_2", units = 16, activation = 'relu') %>%
  
    layer_dense(units = 1)
summary(model_pca_neural)
## Model: "sequential_37"
## ________________________________________________________________________________
##  Layer (type)                  Output Shape               Param #    Trainable  
## ================================================================================
##  normalization_37 (Normalizat  (None, 7)                  15         Y          
##  ion)                                                                           
##  HiddenLayer_1 (Dense)         (None, 32)                 256        Y          
##  HiddenLayer_2 (Dense)         (None, 16)                 528        Y          
##  dense (Dense)                 (None, 1)                  17         Y          
## ================================================================================
## Total params: 816 (3.19 KB)
## Trainable params: 801 (3.13 KB)
## Non-trainable params: 15 (64.00 Byte)
## ________________________________________________________________________________
model_pca_neural %>% compile(
  loss = "MSE",
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  metrics = "accuracy"
)

model_pca_neural %>% fit(
  x = X,
  y = y,
  epochs = 100,
  verbose = 0
) 

model_pca_neural %>% evaluate(X, y)
## 124/124 - 0s - loss: 0.1323 - accuracy: 0.8101 - 223ms/epoch - 2ms/step
##      loss  accuracy 
## 0.1322606 0.8101490

PCA: Ensemble methods

randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 , data=data,
             mtry=(ncol(data)-1), ntree=500)
## Warning in randomForest.default(m, y, ...): invalid mtry: reset to within valid
## range
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 +      PC6 + PC7, data = data, mtry = (ncol(data) - 1), ntree = 500) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 500
## No. of variables tried at each split: 7
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 24.77%
## Confusion matrix:
##     0    1 class.error
## 0 751  597   0.4428783
## 1 384 2229   0.1469575
model_pca_bag <- randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 , data=data,
                          mtry=(ncol(wine_train)-1), ntree=500)
## Warning in randomForest.default(m, y, ...): invalid mtry: reset to within valid
## range
# Building a random forest model using the randomForest function
# mtry parameter is set to 11, which controls feature subsampling
# ntree parameter is set to 500, specifying the number of trees in the random forest
model_rf <- randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 , data=data, ntree=500, importance=TRUE)
varImpPlot(model_rf, main="Variable Importance for Bagging with PCA ")

Result

In the model with PCA we have a slightly larger OOB error than before of 24.34% . The class errors are also slightly larger than before .

Still the right plot tends to spread variable importance over more inputs than the right plot .

###Boosting

model_pca_gbm <- gbm(quality~PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7,
                 data=data,
                 distribution="gaussian", # Distribution of the response variable , sutiable for regression
                 interaction.depth=1, # maximum depth of variable interactions in any tree set to 1
                 n.trees=500, # the total number of trees to fit
                 shrinkage=0.1, # The learning rate , smaller values generally lead to better performance
                 cv.folds = 5 # 5-fold cross-validation should be used.
)

gbm.perf(model_pca_gbm, method = "cv")

## [1] 227

The plot result now is different from the model without PCA .

The error decreases sharply at first and then stabilizes around 300 ( Slighly larger than before ) iterations.

# Generating a summary of the boosting model, but plotit parameter is set to FALSE, so no plot will be generated
summary(model_pca_gbm, plotit = FALSE)
##     var   rel.inf
## PC1 PC1 27.199016
## PC3 PC3 21.059991
## PC4 PC4 16.494856
## PC5 PC5 11.814381
## PC2 PC2 10.079664
## PC6 PC6  7.076612
## PC7 PC7  6.275480
### Organizing  both bagging and boosting output and display them together on one scale.
# Organizing outputs for plotting
dt_varimp <- merge(data.table(importance(model_pca_bag, type = 2), keep.rownames = TRUE),
                   data.table(summary(model_pca_gbm, plotit = FALSE)),
                   by.x = "rn", by.y = "var")
dt_varimp <- setNames(dt_varimp, c("Variable", "Bagging", "Boosting"))
dt_varimp[, Bagging := 100*Bagging/sum(Bagging)]
dt_varimp <- melt(dt_varimp, id.vars = "Variable",
                  measure.vars = c("Bagging","Boosting"),
                  variable.name = "y",
                  value.name = "x")
dt_varimp <- dt_varimp[order(y,-x)]

# Plotting
ggplot(data = dt_varimp) +
  geom_col(aes(y=Variable, x=x, fill=y)) +
  labs(
    x = "Relative Importance",
    y = "Variables") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#f1b147","#4CA7DE")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_line(color = "#000000"),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~y)

rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf"))
## Warning in rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf")): object 'gbm.perf' not found
rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf"))
## Warning in rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf")): object 'dt_varimp' not found
## Warning in rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf")): object 'gbm.perf' not found

Models with full PCA

Full PCA: Logistic Regression

# Define a logistic model using all PCAs
model_pca_full_glm <- glm(formula = quality~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11,
                 data = data, family = binomial(link = "logit"))

# Print summary statistic
print(summary(model_pca_full_glm))
## 
## Call:
## glm(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + 
##     PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
##     data = data)
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
## (Intercept)  0.89490    0.04214  21.236  < 2e-16 ***
## PC1          0.44925    0.02387  18.820  < 2e-16 ***
## PC2         -0.10595    0.03077  -3.443 0.000576 ***
## PC3         -0.63036    0.04057 -15.539  < 2e-16 ***
## PC4          0.39533    0.03902  10.132  < 2e-16 ***
## PC5          0.16162    0.04032   4.008 6.12e-05 ***
## PC6         -0.10274    0.04141  -2.481 0.013095 *  
## PC7          0.19037    0.04807   3.960 7.49e-05 ***
## PC8          0.53393    0.05226  10.217  < 2e-16 ***
## PC9         -0.91491    0.06269 -14.593  < 2e-16 ***
## PC10         0.23926    0.07454   3.210 0.001328 ** 
## PC11        -0.70634    0.30153  -2.343 0.019153 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
## 
##     Null deviance: 5080.0  on 3960  degrees of freedom
## Residual deviance: 3981.2  on 3949  degrees of freedom
## AIC: 4005.2
## 
## Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5
# Fit of the model
with(model_pca_full_glm, pchisq(null.deviance - deviance, df.null - df.residual, lower.tail = FALSE))
## [1] 1.04823e-228

Still a p-value below 0.001 indicates that the model performs better than an empty model

Full PCA: Tree

### Base tree
data$quality <- as.factor(data$quality)
# Build a decision tree model using rpart
model_tree <- rpart(data = data, formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, method = "class")
# Plot the decision tree model using prp, with extra details (extra = 2)
prp(model_tree, extra = 2, main = "Base tree for model")

# Print an plot data on tree
printcp(model_tree)
## 
## Classification tree:
## rpart(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + 
##     PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, data = data, method = "class")
## 
## Variables actually used in tree construction:
## [1] PC1 PC3 PC8 PC9
## 
## Root node error: 1348/3961 = 0.34032
## 
## n= 3961 
## 
##         CP nsplit rel error  xerror     xstd
## 1 0.044758      0   1.00000 1.00000 0.022122
## 2 0.031899      3   0.86573 0.93101 0.021722
## 3 0.023739      4   0.83383 0.91320 0.021608
## 4 0.021513      5   0.81009 0.90504 0.021555
## 5 0.018546      6   0.78858 0.89985 0.021520
## 6 0.010000      8   0.75148 0.84570 0.021138
plotcp(model_tree)

# Determine the minimum complexity parameter (cp) for pruning
#cp parameter: captures model complexity
min_cp <- model_tree$cptable[which.min(model_tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
# Prune the decision tree model using the minimum cp
model_tree_2 <- prune(model_tree, cp = min_cp)
# Plot the pruned decision tree model using prp.
prp(model_tree_2, main = "Pruned base tree with less complexity")

# Calculate the mean accuracy of the model predictions
mean(predict(model_tree, data, type = "class")==data$quality)
## [1] 0.7442565
mean(predict(model_tree_2, data, type = "class")==data$quality)
## [1] 0.7442565

In this case the pruned and un-pruned model are identical which makes sense given the nature of PCA.

model_pca_full_trees <- model_tree
rm(list = c("model_tree", "model_tree_2"))

Full PCA: Neural Network

set.seed(12345)

#Setting up Data
X <- as.matrix(data[,c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3", "PC4","PC5", "PC6", "PC7", "PC8", "PC9", "PC10", "PC11")])
y <- as.matrix(as.numeric(as.character(data$quality)))

normalize_pca_full <- layer_normalization()
normalize_pca_full %>% adapt(X)

print(normalize_pca_full$mean)
## tf.Tensor(
## [[-7.1711838e-08  3.7252903e-08 -2.2118911e-08  1.2165401e-08
##    2.7648639e-10 -1.1350494e-08  3.2596290e-09  6.1234459e-08
##   -3.7252903e-09  2.4447218e-09  2.3865141e-09]], shape=(1, 11), dtype=float32)
model_pca_full_neural <- keras_model_sequential()

model_pca_full_neural %>% 
  normalize_pca_full() %>%
  
  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_1", units = 32, activation = 'relu') %>%   
  layer_dense(name = "HiddenLayer_2", units = 16, activation = 'relu') %>%
  
    layer_dense(units = 1)

summary(model_pca_full_neural)
## Model: "sequential_38"
## ________________________________________________________________________________
##  Layer (type)                  Output Shape               Param #    Trainable  
## ================================================================================
##  normalization_38 (Normalizat  (None, 11)                 23         Y          
##  ion)                                                                           
##  HiddenLayer_1 (Dense)         (None, 32)                 384        Y          
##  HiddenLayer_2 (Dense)         (None, 16)                 528        Y          
##  dense_1 (Dense)               (None, 1)                  17         Y          
## ================================================================================
## Total params: 952 (3.72 KB)
## Trainable params: 929 (3.63 KB)
## Non-trainable params: 23 (96.00 Byte)
## ________________________________________________________________________________
model_pca_full_neural %>% compile(
  loss = "MSE",
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  metrics = "accuracy"
) 

model_pca_full_neural %>% fit(
  x = X,
  y = y,
  epochs = 100,
  verbose = 0
)

model_pca_full_neural %>% evaluate(X, y)
## 124/124 - 0s - loss: 0.1183 - accuracy: 0.8422 - 223ms/epoch - 2ms/step
##      loss  accuracy 
## 0.1182900 0.8422115

Full PCA: Ensemble methods

randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, data=data,
             mtry=(ncol(data)-1), ntree=500)
## Warning in randomForest.default(m, y, ...): invalid mtry: reset to within valid
## range
## 
## Call:
##  randomForest(formula = quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 +      PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, data = data, mtry = (ncol(data) -      1), ntree = 500) 
##                Type of random forest: classification
##                      Number of trees: 500
## No. of variables tried at each split: 11
## 
##         OOB estimate of  error rate: 22.67%
## Confusion matrix:
##     0    1 class.error
## 0 812  536   0.3976261
## 1 362 2251   0.1385381
model_pca_full_bag <- randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, data=data,
                              mtry=(ncol(wine_train)-1), ntree=500)


# Building a random forest model using the randomForest function
# mtry parameter is set to 11, which controls feature subsampling
# ntree parameter is set to 500, specifying the number of trees in the random forest
model_rf <- randomForest(quality ~ PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11, data=data, ntree=500, importance=TRUE)
varImpPlot(model_rf, main="Variable Importance for Bagging with  FULL PCA ")

In the model with full PCA we have a much similar OOB error with the model , then when we used 7 PCA .

###Boosting

model_pca_full_gbm <- gbm(quality~PC1 + PC2 + PC3 + PC4 + PC5 + PC6 + PC7 + PC8 + PC9 + PC10 + PC11,
                     data=data,
                     distribution="gaussian", # Distribution of the response variable , sutiable for regression
                     interaction.depth=1, # maximum depth of variable interactions in any tree set to 1
                     n.trees=500, # the total number of trees to fit
                     shrinkage=0.1, # The learning rate , smaller values generally lead to better performance
                     cv.folds = 5 # 5-fold cross-validation should be used.
)

gbm.perf(model_pca_full_gbm, method = "cv")

## [1] 199

The error decreases sharply at first and then stabilizes around 220 ( Slighly larger than before ) iterations.

# Generating a summary of the boosting model, but plotit parameter is set to FALSE, so no plot will be generated
summary(model_pca_full_gbm, plotit = FALSE)
##       var   rel.inf
## PC1   PC1 21.371654
## PC9   PC9 16.755573
## PC3   PC3 15.753684
## PC4   PC4  8.918292
## PC2   PC2  7.234359
## PC8   PC8  7.136680
## PC5   PC5  5.905786
## PC11 PC11  5.553712
## PC6   PC6  4.793346
## PC7   PC7  4.081825
## PC10 PC10  2.495090
### Organizing  both bagging and boosting output and display them together on one scale.
# Organizing outputs for plotting
dt_varimp <- merge(data.table(importance(model_pca_full_bag, type = 2), keep.rownames = TRUE),
                   data.table(summary(model_pca_full_gbm, plotit = FALSE)),
                   by.x = "rn", by.y = "var")
dt_varimp <- setNames(dt_varimp, c("Variable", "Bagging", "Boosting"))
dt_varimp[, Bagging := 100*Bagging/sum(Bagging)]
dt_varimp <- melt(dt_varimp, id.vars = "Variable",
                  measure.vars = c("Bagging","Boosting"),
                  variable.name = "y",
                  value.name = "x")
dt_varimp <- dt_varimp[order(y,-x)]

# Plotting
ggplot(data = dt_varimp) +
  geom_col(aes(y=Variable, x=x, fill=y)) +
  labs(
    x = "Relative Importance",
    y = "Variables") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#f1b147","#4CA7DE")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.line = element_line(color = "#000000"),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        legend.position = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~y)

rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf"))
## Warning in rm(list = c("dt_varimp", "gbm.perf")): object 'gbm.perf' not found

Using 11 PCs our bagging model performs the same as the bagging model using only the first 7 PCs.

Validation Data

test_solution<-read.csv("C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/test_solution.csv")
test_data<-read.csv("C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_test.csv")

test_solution$quality <- as.factor(test_solution$quality)

test_data$quality <- as.factor <-(test_data$quality)
test_data$quality <- test_solution$quality


test_data <- apply_pca(test_data, model_pca)

test_solution <- as.data.frame(test_data$quality)
colnames(test_solution) <- "quality"
# Prediction for logistic regression
test_solution$logistic_prediction <- predict(model_glm,
                                             newdata = test_data)
# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$logistic_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$logistic_prediction > 0.5 , 1, 0)

# Prediction for tree regression
test_solution$tree_prediction <- predict(model_trees,
                                         newdata = test_data)[,"1"]
# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$tree_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$tree_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for Neural network
test_solution$neural_predicton <- predict(model_neural, 
                                          x = as.matrix(test_data[, c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar", "chlorides", "free.sulfur.dioxide", "total.sulfur.dioxide", "density", "pH", "sulphates", "alcohol")]), verbose = 0)

# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$neural_predicton <- as.numeric(test_solution$neural_predicton)
test_solution$neural_predicton <- ifelse(test_solution$neural_predicton > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for bagging
test_solution$bagging_prediction <- predict(model_bag,
                                            newdata = test_data)

# Prediction for boosting
test_solution$boosting_prediction <- predict(model_gbm,
                                             newdata = test_data)
## Using 197 trees...
test_solution$boosting_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$boosting_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$boosting_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$boosting_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

##PCA

# Prediction for logistic regression
test_solution$logistic_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_glm,
                                                 newdata = test_data)
test_solution$logistic_pca_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$logistic_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for tree regression
test_solution$tree_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_trees,
                                             newdata = test_data)[,"1"]

test_solution$tree_pca_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$tree_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for Neural network
test_solution$neural_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_neural, 
                                              x = as.matrix(test_data[,c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3", "PC4", "PC5", "PC6", "PC7")]), verbose = 0)

# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$neural_pca_prediction <- as.numeric(test_solution$neural_pca_prediction)
test_solution$neural_pca_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$neural_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for bagging
test_solution$bagging_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_bag,
                                                newdata = test_data)


# Prediction for boosting
test_solution$boosting_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_gbm,
                                                 newdata = test_data)
## Using 227 trees...
test_solution$boosting_pca_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$boosting_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

##Full PCA

# Prediction for logistic regression
test_solution$logistic_pca_full_prediction <- predict(model_pca_full_glm,
                                                      newdata = test_data)
test_solution$logistic_pca_full_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$logistic_pca_full_prediction > 0, 1, 0)

# Prediction for tree regression
test_solution$tree_pca_full_prediction <- predict(model_pca_full_trees,
                                                  newdata = test_data)[,"1"]
test_solution$tree_pca_full_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$tree_pca_full_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for Neural network
test_solution$neural_pca_full_prediction <- predict(model_pca_full_neural, x = as.matrix(test_data[,c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3", "PC4", "PC5", "PC6", "PC7", "PC8", "PC9","PC10","PC11")]))
## 25/25 - 0s - 111ms/epoch - 4ms/step
# Converting the predicted probabilities to binary predictions (0 or 1) using a threshold of 0.5
test_solution$neural_pca_full_prediction <- as.numeric(test_solution$neural_pca_full_prediction)
test_solution$neural_pca_full_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$neural_pca_full_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Prediction for bagging
test_solution$bagging_pca_full_prediction <- predict(model_pca_full_bag,
                                                     newdata = test_data)


# Prediction for boosting
test_solution$boosting_pca_full_prediction <- predict(model_pca_full_gbm,
                                                      newdata = test_data)
## Using 199 trees...
test_solution$boosting_pca_full_prediction <- ifelse(test_solution$boosting_pca_full_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)

Validation

# Calculating correct guesses for a column
# Defining a function that calculates the overall accuracy of predictions for a given column by comparing it with the true 'quality' values.
calculate_correct_guesses <- function(column, quality) {
  sum(column == quality) / length(column)
}

# Calculate correct guesses for each column
# The 'sapply()' function applies the 'calculate_correct_guesses()' function to each column of 'test_solution'.
# The result is the overall accuracy of predictions for each column.
correct_guesses <- sapply(test_solution[-1], calculate_correct_guesses, test_solution$quality)
correct_guesses <- c(1, correct_guesses)


# Add the three rows to the original dataframe
# Create a new data frame 'solution' with the calculated accuracy values and corresponding column names.
solution <- data.frame("Accuracy" = correct_guesses)
solution
##                               Accuracy
##                              1.0000000
## logistic_prediction          0.7310606
## tree_prediction              0.7474747
## neural_predicton             0.7474747
## bagging_prediction           0.7651515
## boosting_prediction          0.6527778
## logistic_pca_prediction      0.7159091
## tree_pca_prediction          0.7260101
## neural_pca_prediction        0.8093434
## bagging_pca_prediction       0.9343434
## boosting_pca_prediction      0.6527778
## logistic_pca_full_prediction 0.7247475
## tree_pca_full_prediction     0.7222222
## neural_pca_full_prediction   0.8472222
## bagging_pca_full_prediction  0.9608586
## boosting_pca_full_prediction 0.6527778

Cross-Validation

#Merge training and test data for cross-validation 
wine_train <- read.csv("C:/Users/DELL/Desktop/wine_train.csv")
wine_train_pca <- predict(model_pca, wine_train)  # pca_model is your PCA model used for test_data

# Adding PCA columns to wine_train
wine_train <- cbind(wine_train, wine_train_pca)
full_data <- rbind(wine_train, test_data)
# Check if data was merged before
if (exists("full_data")) {
  cv_data <- full_data
} else {
  cv_data <- wine_train
}

# Define a function to split data into K folds
# Assign a fold number to each row randomly using 'ceiling' and 'sample'.
split_K <- function(dt, folds){
  dt$id <- ceiling(sample(1:nrow(dt), replace = FALSE, nrow(dt)) / 
                     (nrow(dt) / folds))
  return(dt)
}

# Set the number of folds (K) for cross-validation
K <- 10

cv_data <- split_K(cv_data, K)
# Create a data frame 'cross_validation' to store accuracy values for each model and fold
cross_validation <- data.frame(matrix(NA, nrow = 10, ncol = K))
rownames(cross_validation) <- c("Logistic", "Tree Model", "Neural Network",
                                "Bagging", "Boosting", "PCA Logistic", "PCA Tree Model", "PCA Neural Network",
                                "PCA Bagging", "PCA Boosting")

for (i in 1:K) {
 
  dt <- cv_data[cv_data$id == i, ]
  
   # Prediction for logistic regression
  dt$logistic_prediction <- predict(model_glm, 
                                               newdata = dt)
  dt$logistic_prediction <- ifelse(dt$logistic_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[1, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$logistic_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for tree regression
  dt$tree_prediction <- predict(model_trees, 
                                           newdata = dt)[,"1"]
  dt$tree_prediction <- ifelse(dt$tree_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[2, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$tree_prediction, dt$quality)

  # Prediction for bagging
  dt$bagging_prediction <- predict(model_bag, 
                                              newdata = dt)
 
  
  cross_validation[3, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$bagging_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for boosting
  dt$boosting_prediction <- predict(model_gbm, 
                                               newdata = dt)
  dt$boosting_prediction <- ifelse(dt$boosting_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[4, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$boosting_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for neural network
  dt$neural_prediction <- predict(model_neural, 
                                           x = as.matrix(dt[,c("fixed.acidity", "volatile.acidity", "citric.acid", "residual.sugar", "chlorides", "free.sulfur.dioxide", "total.sulfur.dioxide", "density", "pH", "sulphates", "alcohol")]))
  dt$neural_prediction <- as.numeric(dt$neural_prediction)
  dt$neural_prediction <- ifelse(dt$neural_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[5, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$neural_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  ##PCA
  
  # Prediction for logistic regression
  dt$logistic_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_glm, 
                                               newdata = dt)
  dt$logistic_pca_prediction <- ifelse(dt$logistic_pca_prediction > 0, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[6, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$logistic_pca_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for tree regression
  dt$tree_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_trees, 
                                           newdata = dt)[,"1"]
  dt$tree_pca_prediction <- ifelse(dt$tree_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[7, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$tree_pca_prediction, dt$quality)
  

  
  # Prediction for bagging
  dt$bagging_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_bag, 
                                              newdata = dt)
 
   
  cross_validation[8, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$bagging_pca_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for boosting
  dt$boosting_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_gbm, 
                                               newdata = dt)
  dt$boosting_pca_prediction <- ifelse(dt$boosting_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[9, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$boosting_pca_prediction, dt$quality)
  
  # Prediction for neural network
  dt$neural_pca_prediction <- predict(model_pca_neural, 
                                           x = as.matrix(dt[,c("PC1", "PC2", "PC3", "PC4", "PC5", "PC6", "PC7")]))
  dt$neural_pca_prediction <- as.numeric(dt$neural_pca_prediction)
  dt$neural_pca_prediction <- ifelse(dt$neural_pca_prediction > 0.5, 1, 0)
  
  cross_validation[10, i] <- calculate_correct_guesses(dt$neural_pca_prediction, dt$quality)
  
}
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 36ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...

## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 38ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 33ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 32ms/epoch - 2ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 37ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 37ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 38ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 35ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 39ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 34ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 38ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 40ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 79ms/epoch - 6ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 86ms/epoch - 7ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 45ms/epoch - 3ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 48ms/epoch - 4ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 56ms/epoch - 4ms/step
## Using 197 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 52ms/epoch - 4ms/step
## Using 227 trees...
## 13/13 - 0s - 68ms/epoch - 5ms/step
cross_validation$Mean_accuracy <- rowMeans(cross_validation)
cross_validation$Expected_Loss <- (1 - cross_validation$Mean_accuracy)
cross_validation[, c(11, 12)]
##                    Mean_accuracy Expected_Loss
## Logistic               0.7447676    0.25523243
## Tree Model             0.7647082    0.23529184
## Neural Network         0.9532994    0.04670063
## Bagging                0.6596806    0.34031944
## Boosting               0.8159663    0.18403366
## PCA Logistic           0.6702853    0.32971465
## PCA Tree Model         0.6743238    0.32567616
## PCA Neural Network     0.4577125    0.54228748
## PCA Bagging            0.6596806    0.34031944
## PCA Boosting           0.4322151    0.56778490

Above table indicates Mean_accuracy and Expected_Loss for each model. Mean_accuracy, representing the average accuracy of the model across all the 10 folds in the cross-validation process. So, it shows how well the model performs on average in predicting the quality. On the other hand, Expected_Loss indicates the proportion of misclassified instances. In short, the lower the expected loss, the better the performance of the model. Therefore, after cross-validation we find that Neural Network without PCA seems to perform best, followed by Boosting without PCA.
